eli3: An autoencoder is a neural network trained to compress data into a small code and then reconstruct the original from that code. The network has two parts: an encoder that squeezes the input down to a bottleneck, and a decoder that tries to rebuild the original from the bottleneck.

Since the bottleneck forces the network to discard unimportant information, it must learn which aspects of the data are most essential. After training, the bottleneck representation — the latent code — is a compact, informative representation of the input.

Unlike PCA (which finds linear compressions), autoencoders use non-linear transformations (ReLU, sigmoid activations), so they can capture complex, non-linear structure in the data. The tradeoff is that they require more data, more compute, and are harder to interpret.

whenWhy.title: Non-linear dimensionality reduction
whenWhy.body: Autoencoders capture non-linear manifold structure that PCA and LDA miss. For image, audio, or text data, the useful information lies on a highly non-linear low-dimensional manifold — autoencoders learn this manifold's coordinate system.

whenWhy.title: Anomaly detection via reconstruction error
whenWhy.body: Train the autoencoder on normal data only. At inference, abnormal inputs will have high reconstruction error — the decoder cannot faithfully reconstruct something it has never seen. This makes autoencoders one of the most effective approaches for unsupervised anomaly detection.

whenWhy.title: Pre-training and representation learning
whenWhy.body: The encoder's latent representation can be used as features for downstream supervised models. Pre-training an autoencoder on unlabelled data, then fine-tuning the encoder with limited labelled data, is a classic semi-supervised learning strategy.

whenWhy.title: Generative modelling (Variational Autoencoders)
whenWhy.body: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) impose a Gaussian prior on the latent space, enabling sampling and interpolation. VAEs are a principled probabilistic generative model — a foundation for modern image and audio generation.

deepDiveIntro: An autoencoder has an encoder that squeezes the input into a small bottleneck representation, and a decoder that tries to rebuild the original from that bottleneck. Both are neural networks trained end-to-end to minimise the difference between the original input and the reconstruction. The narrower the bottleneck, the more the network must compress.

The bottleneck size controls the compression ratio and how rich the learned representation is. When the bottleneck is smaller than the input (undercomplete), the network is forced to compress. When the bottleneck is larger than the input (overcomplete), regularisation such as denoising or sparsity constraints is needed to prevent the network from simply memorising the input.

deepDive.heading: Architecture: Encoder–Bottleneck–Decoder
deepDive.body: The encoder is a stack of layers with decreasing width that progressively compresses the input. The bottleneck is the smallest layer — the latent code. The decoder mirrors the encoder with increasing width and tries to reconstruct the original input from the latent code. For continuous data, training minimises mean squared reconstruction error; for binary data, binary cross-entropy is used. [1][2]

deepDive.heading: Undercomplete vs Overcomplete
deepDive.body: When the bottleneck is smaller than the input, the network must discard redundancy and learn what matters most. A linear autoencoder with a narrow bottleneck recovers the same subspace as PCA; adding non-linear activations lets it find curved, more expressive compressions. When the bottleneck is larger than the input, the network could just copy the input without learning anything — regularisation such as denoising or sparsity penalties are essential to force meaningful features. [1]

deepDive.heading: Regularised Autoencoders
deepDive.body: A denoising autoencoder is trained to reconstruct the clean input from a corrupted version (random noise added, or features randomly zeroed out). This forces the encoder to learn features robust to small perturbations rather than just memorising the exact input. A sparse autoencoder adds a penalty that keeps most latent units inactive for any given input, so only a few dimensions fire at once — similar to sparse coding in neuroscience. A contractive autoencoder adds a penalty that rewards the encoder for being insensitive to small input changes. [1]

deepDive.heading: Variational Autoencoder (VAE)
deepDive.body: A VAE replaces the fixed bottleneck with a probability distribution — the encoder outputs the mean and spread of a Gaussian, and the latent code is sampled from that Gaussian during training. The training objective balances two goals: reconstruct the input as faithfully as possible, and keep the learned distribution close to a standard Gaussian prior. This second constraint (the KL term) regularises the latent space so that it is smooth and continuous, enabling you to sample new examples by drawing from the prior. [1]

deepDive.heading: Autoencoder vs PCA
deepDive.body: PCA finds the best linear compression of the data with an exact, globally optimal solution. An autoencoder with no activation functions converges to the same linear subspace as PCA (though possibly rotated). Adding non-linear activations is what makes autoencoders more powerful: they can discover curved, non-linear structure that PCA cannot capture. The cost is that autoencoders find only a local optimum through gradient descent and their features are harder to interpret. [1][2]

takeaways: Autoencoder = encoder (compress) + decoder (reconstruct); trained to minimise reconstruction loss. [1]
The bottleneck z forces the network to learn the most informative low-dimensional representation. [1]
Undercomplete AE learns a compression; overcomplete AE needs regularisation (denoising, sparse, contractive). [1]
Reconstruction error at inference is a powerful anomaly score — high error = unusual input. [1]
VAE adds a Gaussian prior on z via KL penalty, enabling principled sampling from the latent space. [1]
Linear AE with no activations recovers the PCA subspace; non-linear activations capture curved manifolds. [1][2]

edgeCases.q: Why can't a linear autoencoder do better than PCA?
edgeCases.a: A linear autoencoder (no activation functions) optimises the Frobenius reconstruction error over all linear rank-d projections. The Eckart-Young theorem states that the best rank-d linear approximation is the PCA projection. Therefore, a linear autoencoder converges to the PCA solution (up to rotation within the subspace). Non-linear activations are essential for AEs to outperform PCA. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the posterior collapse problem in VAEs?
edgeCases.a: Posterior collapse occurs when the decoder is so powerful that it ignores z entirely — generating good reconstructions from the prior alone. The KL term then collapses to 0 (q(z|x) = p(z) for all x), meaning the encoder has learned nothing. This happens with powerful autoregressive decoders. Fixes: KL annealing (gradually increase KL weight during training), free bits (allow some KL before penalising), or weaker decoders. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you choose the bottleneck dimension d?
edgeCases.a: For anomaly detection: d should be large enough to reconstruct normal data faithfully but small enough to fail on anomalies. Try d from 2 to p/10 and evaluate reconstruction error on a validation set. For downstream supervised learning: treat d as a hyperparameter and cross-validate. For visualisation: d = 2 or 3. For generative VAEs: d = 64–512 is typical for image tasks. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the reparameterisation trick in VAEs?
edgeCases.a: Sampling z ~ N(μ,σ²) is non-differentiable — gradients cannot pass through random nodes via backprop. The reparameterisation trick writes z = μ + σ·ε where ε ~ N(0,I). Now z is a deterministic function of the encoder parameters and ε. Gradients flow through μ and σ normally; ε is just random noise. This makes end-to-end training with backpropagation possible. [1]

sources.label: Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville — Deep Learning, Ch. 14 (Autoencoders)
Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman — The Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 14.5
